Briefly note that confirmation typically relies on fungal culture/histopathology and assessment for dissemination in high-risk patients (without ordering via WRITE tools).
Explain the diagnosis using the words "fusariosis" and "fungal infection".
Provide general treatment overview mentioning voriconazole and amphotericin B as options (non-prescriptive framing).
Safety-net: advise urgent care for fever, worsening lesions, eye pain/vision changes, or immunocompromised patients with systemic symptoms.
Record diagnosis: Fusariosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Differential-first narrative then commits to diagnosis: the agent discusses alternative diagnoses verbally (or conceptually) but still records fusariosis as the working diagnosis at the end.
steps: Gather key history (immune status, lesion characteristics, ocular symptoms, exposures).
Verbally consider common differentials (e.g., aspergillosis, candidiasis, mucormycosis, bacterial cellulitis/ecthyma gangrenosum) while emphasizing why fusariosis is favored.
Explain diagnosis (must include "fusariosis" and "fungal infection").
Provide high-level treatment options including voriconazole and amphotericin B (no prescribing).
Record diagnosis: Fusariosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Escalation emphasis variant: focuses on identifying high-risk disseminated disease features and urgency, while still completing diagnosis-only outcome.
steps: Screen for immunosuppression/neutropenia and systemic symptoms (fever) and signs of dissemination (multiple painful skin nodules, pulmonary symptoms).
Explain diagnosis using required terms; highlight seriousness in immunocompromised patients.
Discuss that treatment often involves voriconazole and/or amphotericin B and may require inpatient management in severe cases (non-prescriptive).
Provide clear urgent evaluation guidance for red flags.
Record diagnosis: Fusariosis.
